ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for low-carb chicken yakitori bowl. FOODON_00002473 (chicken dish) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (grilled chicken meat) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a composite yakitori bowl with tofu and salad.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl of approximately 400 g, comprising charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori skewers, firm tofu, and fresh salad greens with sesame dressing, served without rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477) and Tofu, firm, prepared with calcium sulfate (FDC ID 172475), combined with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: The input per_100g values (energy 100 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 5.56 g, carbohydrate 3.33 g) are consistent with a composite dish of grilled chicken thigh (~55–60% by weight), firm tofu (~20%), and mixed salad greens (~20%) with yakitori sauce. USDA FDC grilled chicken thigh (per 100g: ~177 kcal, 25.1 g protein, 7.8 g fat) and firm tofu (per 100g: ~76 kcal, 8.1 g protein, 4.8 g fat) were used to validate the blended per_100g values from the input. The input values are plausible for this composite at the stated proportions. Secondary micronutrient values (calcium 33.33 mg, iron 0.67 mg, cholesterol 55.56 mg, sodium 266.67 mg) are consistent with USDA FDC component data. Values retained from input with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on grilled chicken (GI not applicable, negligible carbohydrate) and firm tofu (GI ~15, low) and mixed salad vegetables (GI ~15–32); composite GI estimated at ~35 weighted by carbohydrate contribution.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite low-carb chicken yakitori bowl. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: firm tofu (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database), mixed salad vegetables and tomato (GI ~15–32, Atkinson et al. 2008), and yakitori sauce (mirin/sugar, GI ~55–60, minor contributor at low volume). Weighted composite GI ~35. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 3.33 g carbohydrate per 100g × 4) / 100 = 4.7 g, classified as very low GL. This is a conservative estimate; actual GI may be lower given the high protein and fat content of the dish, which further blunts glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 266.7 mg per 100 g; estimated 1067 mg per 400 g bowl, approaching 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium yakitori sauce and miso, ask for sesame dressing on the side, and limit portion to half a bowl to keep sodium intake within safe limits for a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 3.33 g per 100 g (13.3 g per 400 g bowl) and low estimated GI of ~35 result in a glycemic load of approximately 4.7 g per serving, which is clinically negligible
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and high protein; individuals should still request light yakitori sauce application to minimise added sugar from mirin.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 55.56 mg per 100 g (222 mg per 400 g bowl) and sodium at 266.7 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.33 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken yakitori to reduce cholesterol and saturated fat, request reduced-sodium sauce and miso, and limit portion size to manage daily sodium and dietary cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Carb Chicken Yakitori Bowl is a Japanese-inspired dish sold in Singapore, consisting of charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori skewers served over a bed of fresh mixed salad greens and firm tofu, replacing the traditional rice base with a low-carbohydrate alternative. The chicken is marinated and grilled in yakitori style using a tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, imparting a savoury-sweet caramelised flavour with light smokiness from charcoal grilling. The firm tofu provides a neutral, slightly dense texture that contrasts with the tender grilled chicken. The salad base typically includes mixed greens, tomato, and seaweed, dressed with sesame dressing. The dish is designed for health-conscious consumers seeking a protein-rich, low-carbohydrate meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 100 kcal, 10 g protein, 5.56 g fat, and only 3.33 g carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carb and ketogenic dietary patterns. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 35 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 4.7 per 400 g serving, making it clinically appropriate for type 2 diabetes management. Sodium content is moderate at 266.7 mg per 100 g, which warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is dairy-free and contains soy, sesame, and gluten allergens from the yakitori sauce and miso components.
common_user_queries: Low carb Japanese chicken bowl Singapore
Is chicken yakitori bowl good for diabetes?
Japanese grilled chicken bowl no rice

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are from GrabFood (delivery platform, a forbidden primary source) with confidence 0.7. Values have been cross-validated against USDA FDC component data for grilled chicken thigh and firm tofu and found to be plausible for the stated composite dish. No exact approved-source match exists for this specific composite dish. Confidence is therefore medium: values are defensible by component analogy but not directly confirmed by a single approved primary source entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific composite low-carb chicken yakitori bowl are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16 for this exact dish formulation. Input source (GrabFood) is a forbidden primary source and was not used as the nutrient authority; USDA FDC component analogy was used instead. The input ethnic_tags contained 'Beef Dish' and 'Japanese Curry' which are clearly erroneous for this chicken dish and have been removed. Two portions in the input both had is_default: true; the 400 g bowl has been designated the canonical default serving. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Trans fat retained at 0.02 g per 100 g from input, consistent with trace levels in grilled chicken; this is a trace value consistent with USDA FDC data for grilled chicken thigh.
